So this is a brief update on a draft about API keys and privacy that has started some time ago.
Um, originally on a blog post.
Oh, this was inspired by some blog posts about, um, how API keys can get leaked over unencrypted HTTP.

Um, if you imagine the following scenario that you have some client that connects to an API, um and wants to authenticate using some API, but in some circumstances, whether it be developer error or just misconfiguration or miscommunication, um, it can happen that the key gets sent over unencrypted HTTP.
Um, what is very common, especially in browser deployments, for example, is that those unencrypted HTTP endpoints then redirect to Https, which is in theory great because then you're encrypted.
But if the client just blindly follows it and sends the request again, it gets a successful response back.
And from the developer or the client perspective, everything's fine.
They get a successful response back.
They don't investigate anymore.
But what basically happened is that the API keys were exposed over plain HTTP and could have been leaked anywhere, um, without the client ever noticing this.
Um, this isn't ideal.
So we started thinking about how can this be prevented?
How can this be mitigated if it happened?
And this graph basically lays out some recommendations for this.
Um, there are some recommendations for the server side.
Um, there are some mechanisms to prevent this.
For example the https DNS record.
It's a small entry in the DNS entries, um, saying like hey, please only connect over HTTP.
There's also HSTs.
Um, but that only works if you Connected ones already.
Um, and also requires the client to, um, store that you have set the HSTs header, um, for cookies.
There's the secure attribute that you can set to make sure they're only sent over Https.
Um, or you could go even harder routes and say you block port 80 entirely and don't accept any HTTP traffic at all.
This might not work depending on your deployment.
Um, it's a rather harsh one, but, um, it works if you really want to prevent this.
Um, if there's no connection established, no API keys can be leaked over it.

Um, setup.
Um, and in this case, um, you should also, um, revoke any credentials that were sent over unencrypted http.
Um, then even if they were leaked, there's no damage anymore.
